While English can sometimes omit a repeated object in a compound predicate, its omission here makes the phrase slightly awkward and momentarily ambiguous. Restoring the pronoun ('them') improves clarity and naturalness without adding unnecessary length, and it more closely reflects the structure of the source text.

revisions.from: and have sent you away in peace
revisions.to: and sent you away in peace
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew verbs 'עָשִׂינוּ' (we did/have done) and 'וַנְּשַׁלֵּחֲךָ' (and we sent) form a tight sequence. In English, this is most naturally rendered as a compound predicate governed by a single auxiliary verb: 'we have done... and sent...'. Repeating 'have' is grammatically correct but slightly clunky and less fluid than the Hebrew construction it represents. Removing the second 'have' creates a smoother, more idiomatic English sentence.

revisions.from: So he built an altar there and called on the name of the LORD, and he pitched his tent there.
revisions.to: So he built an altar there, called on the name of the LORD, and pitched his tent there.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew text presents three sequential actions by Isaac using parallel verb forms (wayyiqtol). The current English rendering breaks this parallel structure by reintroducing the pronoun 'he' for the third action, which is slightly cumbersome. A simple series ('he built..., called..., and pitched...') would be more concise, improve the narrative flow, and more closely mirror the parallelism of the source text.

revisions.from: looked back from behind him
revisions.to: behind him, looked back
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is a very literal translation of the Hebrew syntax (verb + prepositional phrase), which results in a slightly redundant and awkward English phrase. 'Looked back' already implies the direction of the action. The phrase 'from behind him' specifies her location relative to Lot. Separating these two elements, as in 'But his wife, behind him, looked back,' is more idiomatic and clearer in English while retaining all the information from the source text.

revisions.from: and they shut the door
revisions.to: and shut the door
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew presents a rapid sequence of actions. Using a compound predicate in English ('...brought Lot... and shut the door') creates a more fluid and concise sentence that better reflects the pace of the source text. The current rendering, which creates a new independent clause by reintroducing the subject 'they', is grammatically correct but slightly less dynamic.

revisions.from: his hand, and the hand of his wife, and the hands of his two daughters
revisions.to: his hand, his wife’s hand, and his two daughters’ hands
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is faithful to the repetitive structure of the Hebrew but is slightly cumbersome in English. Switching to possessives ('his wife’s hand,' 'his two daughters’ hands') creates a more natural and idiomatic flow for the English reader without any loss of meaning, which aligns well with an optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: bowed down with his face to the ground
revisions.to: bowed with his face to the ground
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה functions as a single idiomatic unit for prostration. The current rendering breaks it down literally, resulting in a slightly redundant English phrase where both 'bowed down' and 'to the ground' indicate downward motion. Removing 'down' creates a more concise and fluid expression ('bowed with his face to the ground') that still captures the full meaning of the Hebrew.

revisions.from: Only do nothing to these men
revisions.to: But to these men, do nothing
revisions.rationale: The construction 'Only do nothing' is slightly unnatural in English. While grammatically correct, it can be momentarily confusing. A phrasing like 'But to these men, do nothing' or 'Just do not do anything to these men' better conveys the contrastive prohibition ('do what you want to my daughters, BUT not to these men') in more fluid English.

revisions.from: A son-in-law
revisions.to: Son-in-law
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew `חָתָן` is an indefinite singular, which the draft renders as 'A son-in-law.' While formally correct, this is slightly awkward in an English list next to the definite possessives 'your sons, your daughters.' It could also be misread to imply Lot has only one son-in-law, which verse 14 shows is not the case. Removing the article ('Son-in-law') treats the word as a category, which better reflects the sense of the Hebrew singular here, avoids the misleading numerical implication, and improves the list's cadence.

revisions.from: that God had spoken to him
revisions.to: of which God had spoken to him
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing 'at the appointed time that God had spoken to him' is ambiguous in English. It could be interpreted as God speaking *at* that time, rather than the time being the subject *of* God's prior speech. The Hebrew relative clause modifies 'the appointed time,' indicating it was the topic of God's earlier promise. The suggested wording, which is reflected in the footnote, clarifies this relationship more precisely.

revisions.from: said to Abraham, saying
revisions.to: said to Abraham
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew infinitive לֵאמֹר (lē'mor) is a common formula for introducing direct speech. While 'saying' is a literal translation, it is redundant in English when following the verb 'said.' Omitting it creates a more natural English sentence without any loss of meaning, which aligns well with an 'optimal-equivalence' philosophy by prioritizing clarity in the target language.

revisions.from: offspring will be named for you
revisions.to: your offspring will be called
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'named for you' is ambiguous in English, as it can mean 'named in your honor.' The Hebrew 'יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע' indicates that the covenant line designated as Abraham's will be reckoned through Isaac. Translating the dative 'לְךָ' as a possessive ('your') and using 'called' (in the sense of 'designated' or 'reckoned') makes the meaning clearer and aligns with the phrasing in NT quotations of this verse (e.g., Rom. 9:7).

revisions.from: and I will wipe out from the face of the ground every living thing that I have made.
revisions.to: and I will wipe out every living thing that I have made from the face of the ground.
revisions.rationale: The current word order places a prepositional phrase between the verb and its long direct object. While grammatically permissible in English, reversing the order to 'verb -> direct object -> prepositional phrase' provides a smoother reading and also happens to align more closely with the Hebrew syntax.

revisions.from: and the three wives of his sons with them
revisions.to: and the three wives of his sons as well
revisions.rationale: The draft's structure is 'Noah entered... with [a list of people].' The Hebrew includes the word אִתָּם ('with them') at the end of this list. Rendering this literally as 'with them' creates a redundancy in English ('with... with them') that is stylistically awkward. A functional equivalent like 'as well' or 'also' preserves the Hebrew's sense of completing the list while being much more natural in English.

revisions.from: among all flesh
revisions.to: of all flesh
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase 'בְּכָל בָּשָׂר' (in/with/of all flesh) functions appositionally to specify the nature of the preceding 'כָּל נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה' (every living creature). The current rendering 'among all flesh' creates a slightly clunky and redundant English phrase ('every living creature among all flesh'). Translating the preposition 'בְּ' as 'of' in this context ('every living creature of all flesh') is more idiomatic in English, aligns with standard renderings, and better captures the specifying relationship between the two Hebrew phrases.

revisions.from: with its life, that is, its blood
revisions.to: with its life—its blood
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses a direct apposition (נַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ) to equate life and blood. While adding 'that is' correctly makes this relationship explicit, it functions more as an explanation than a direct translation, making the English slightly clunky. Using an em-dash ('—') is a common and more elegant English convention for appositives that better captures the concise force of the Hebrew syntax. This construction is already recognized as valid in the provided footnote.

revisions.from: And it happened, at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark that he had made.
revisions.to: At the end of forty days, Noah opened the window of the ark that he had made.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew `וַיְהִי` often introduces a narrative sequence and can be translated as 'And it happened... that...'. However, this construction is somewhat formal and cumbersome in contemporary English. A more natural and direct rendering, which is common in modern optimal-equivalence translations, is to subordinate the introductory temporal clause ('At the end of forty days') and proceed directly to the main action ('Noah opened...'). This improves readability without sacrificing accuracy.

revisions.from: it went out, going back and forth
revisions.to: it went back and forth
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase וַיֵּצֵא יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב (wayyēṣē’ yāṣô’ wāšôḇ) functions as a single idiomatic unit describing a continuous action. The draft's rendering separates the finite verb ('it went out') from the modifying infinitives ('going back and forth'), creating a slightly redundant and clunky English phrase. A more fluid rendering that treats the Hebrew as a single verbal idea, such as 'it went back and forth,' better captures the sense and flow of the original, as seen in many major English translations (e.g., ESV, NET).

revisions.from: As long as all the days of the earth endure
revisions.to: For as long as the earth endures
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is slightly redundant and awkward. The phrase 'as long as' already conveys the sense of duration covered by 'all the days.' A more concise and natural phrasing like 'For as long as the earth endures' or 'While the earth remains' captures the sense of the Hebrew temporal phrase `עֹד כָּל יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ` more idiomatically without losing any meaning. The current version is a slightly clunky hybrid of a literal and a functional translation.

revisions.from: receded from upon the earth, continually receding
revisions.to: continually receded from upon the earth
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing 'receded..., continually receding' is redundant. The Hebrew infinitive absolute construction (הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב) modifies the main verb (וַיָּשֻׁבוּ) to indicate a continuous action. Integrating this modification as an adverb ('continually receded' or 'steadily receded') creates more natural English and avoids the awkward repetition of the same verbal idea, which sounds like 'it walked, continually walking.'

revisions.from: brought her to himself into the ark
revisions.to: brought her into the ark to himself
revisions.rationale: The current rendering directly translates the two Hebrew prepositional phrases ('to him', 'into the ark') in their original order, which is slightly dense and less natural in English. Reordering them to 'into the ark to himself' improves the stylistic flow without losing any of the original meaning.

revisions.from: that is eaten
revisions.to: that is edible
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew Niphal imperfect יֵאָכֵל describes a category of food suitable for consumption. The current rendering 'that is eaten' is slightly awkward in English, as it can imply a present continuous action. 'That is edible' or 'that can be eaten' (as noted in the footnote) more naturally and accurately conveys the sense of suitability for eating.

revisions.from: And Noah did this;
revisions.to: Thus Noah did;
revisions.rationale: The addition of 'this' is an interpretive choice to make the verb 'did' transitive, with the object being the rest of the verse. While the logic is sound, the phrasing 'And Noah did this;' is slightly unnatural as a cataphoric reference in this context. Using 'Thus' or 'So' (e.g., 'Thus Noah did;') better captures the summarizing function of the initial Hebrew clause (וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ) and provides a smoother, more idiomatic transition to the clarifying statement that follows.

revisions.from: because he also is flesh
revisions.to: for he is flesh
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew word בְּשַׁגַּם is a composite form including גַּם ('also'). While translating it literally as 'he also is flesh' is defensible, it creates an awkward English phrase that prompts the reader to ask, 'also what?' The context does not provide a clear comparison. The force of גַּם here is likely intensive, emphasizing the reason. A smoother and more natural rendering like 'for he is flesh' or 'since he is merely flesh' conveys the essential logic without the distracting and unanchored 'also'.

revisions.from: with the earth
revisions.to: and the earth
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew 'אֶת הָאָרֶץ' follows a verb that already has a direct object ('them'). While traditionally rendered 'with the earth,' this is ambiguous in modern English and could imply instrumentality (i.e., that the earth is the weapon). A rendering like 'and the earth' (treating it as a second direct object) or 'along with the earth' (a comitative sense) is grammatically sound and provides greater clarity on the scope of the destruction, which includes both the inhabitants and the earth itself.

revisions.from: And Reu lived, after he fathered Serug, two hundred and seven years,
revisions.to: And Reu lived after he fathered Serug two hundred and seven years,
revisions.rationale: The current punctuation, with commas setting off the temporal phrase 'after he fathered Serug,' unnecessarily breaks up the main clause ('Reu lived... two hundred and seven years'). This makes the sentence feel disjointed. Removing the commas creates a smoother, more natural English sentence that still closely follows the Hebrew word order, which is common in formal-equivalence translations for this type of formula.

revisions.from: lived, after he fathered Peleg, four hundred and thirty years,
revisions.to: lived after he fathered Peleg four hundred and thirty years,
revisions.rationale: The commas around the temporal clause 'after he fathered Peleg' interrupt the sentence flow, making the clause feel parenthetical. This information is essential to the meaning of the duration that follows, not an aside. Removing the commas creates a more natural and direct English sentence that is still faithful to the Hebrew word order and is more grammatically precise in English.

revisions.from: according to their generations, in their nations
revisions.to: according to their genealogies, by their nations
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses two parallel prepositional phrases (לְתוֹלְדֹתָם and בְּגוֹיֵהֶם) to specify the criteria by which the families are listed. The current rendering, with a comma separating the two, feels slightly disjointed. A phrasing like 'according to their genealogies, by their nations' would better reflect the parallel structure and clarifying function of the Hebrew phrases. Using 'genealogies' for תוֹלְדֹתָם is also a strong alternative in this summary context.

revisions.from: My heart is struck down like grass and withered
revisions.to: is struck and withered like grass
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing, while closely following the Hebrew word order, is slightly awkward in English. The Hebrew syntax (supported by the Masoretic accent on כָעֵשֶׂב) links 'like grass' primarily with 'is struck'. However, the poetic sense is that the heart's entire condition—struck and withered—is like that of grass. The current rendering creates a slightly disjointed phrase. The suggested rewrite, 'is struck and withered like grass,' is a common solution (cf. NIV, ESV) that creates a more fluid and powerful image in English by applying the simile to the compound predicate, which better captures the holistic sense of the metaphor.

revisions.from: swarms of flies came, gnats throughout their territory
revisions.to: swarms of flies came, and gnats throughout their territory
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew verse presents two distinct plagues (flies and gnats) in two parallel poetic clauses. The current English rendering, using only a comma to separate them, creates a slight ambiguity. A reader might interpret 'gnats' as an appositive that re-describes the 'swarms of flies,' rather than as a second, separate plague. Adding 'and' clarifies the coordinate relationship between the two plagues, mirroring the parallel structure of the Hebrew and preventing any potential misreading.

revisions.from: repay our neighbors sevenfold into their lap the reproach
revisions.to: repay into the lap of our neighbors sevenfold the reproach
revisions.rationale: The current word order places the prepositional phrase 'into their lap' between the verb's indirect object ('our neighbors') and its direct object ('the reproach'), a structure known as heavy-NP shift. While grammatically permissible, it creates an awkward and slightly stilted cadence in English. The suggested rewrite combines the dative recipient ('to our neighbors') and the destination ('into their lap') into a single, more fluid prepositional phrase ('into the lap of our neighbors'). This improves the flow and clarity of the English sentence without losing any of the Hebrew's meaning.

revisions.from: and do not give him over to the desire of his enemies
revisions.to: and may you not give him over to the desire of his enemies
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew text shifts from a third-person declaration about the LORD to a second-person petition addressed to the LORD (a jussive). The decision to preserve this shift is correct, but the current English rendering 'and do not give him over...' is syntactically jarring. It connects a third-person clause ('he will be called...') with a second-person command using 'and', which reads unnaturally in English. The suggested rewrite, 'and may you not...', preserves the second-person address and the petitionary force of the jussive while using a more conventional and less awkward English construction for prayer or wish.

revisions.from: and they devoured them, and frogs, and they destroyed them
revisions.to: which devoured them, and frogs, which destroyed them
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses singular verbs (וַיֹּאכְלֵם and וַתַּשְׁחִיתֵם) following singular collective nouns. The draft renders the nouns as plurals and then uses plural verbs in coordinate clauses ('and they...'). While this preserves the coordinate structure of the Hebrew, the repetition is slightly awkward in English. Using relative clauses ('which...') is more idiomatic, reads more smoothly, and is the common solution in modern translations for this type of Hebrew construction. It better balances formal features with English readability.

revisions.from: Only for God
revisions.to: For God alone
revisions.rationale: The current rendering 'Only for God' is slightly stilted and potentially ambiguous in English. It could be misread as 'be silent only for the benefit of God'. The Hebrew particle 'אַךְ' here functions to restrict the object, meaning the soul's silent waiting is directed exclusively 'to God'. Translating the phrase as 'For God alone' is more idiomatic and clearly conveys that God is the exclusive object of the exhortation, which is a more precise reflection of the Hebrew syntax and the psalm's theme.

revisions.from: toward God is my soul silent
revisions.to: for God is my soul silent
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'silent toward God' is not idiomatic English for the intended meaning of quiet, trustful waiting. It risks being misinterpreted with a negative connotation, akin to giving someone the 'silent treatment.' The Hebrew preposition 'el' has a broad semantic range, and in this context of dependence and waiting, 'for' is a more natural and clearer rendering. It aligns well with the concept of waiting for salvation, which is explicit in the second clause. This change improves the idiomatic quality without altering the translator's other well-reasoned choices.

revisions.from: Transgression speaks to the wicked within my heart
revisions.to: Within my heart, transgression speaks to the wicked
revisions.rationale: The current word order creates an ambiguity where 'within my heart' could be read as modifying 'the wicked' (i.e., 'the wicked who are in my heart'). While the context of the full verse helps resolve this, the initial phrase is awkward and potentially misleading. Fronting the adverbial phrase ('Within my heart,...') makes it clear that this is the location of the psalmist's perception, which is the intended meaning of the difficult Hebrew text. This minimal reordering preserves the draft's strong lexical choices while improving clarity.

revisions.from: name; holy is he
revisions.to: name, for he is holy.
revisions.rationale: The current rendering creates a grammatical tension in English, as the masculine pronoun 'he' lacks a clear masculine antecedent in the preceding clause (its grammatical antecedent would be the neuter 'name'). The Hebrew is smoother because 'name' (שֵׁם) is a masculine noun, so the pronoun 'הוּא' can grammatically refer to it, even while contextually pointing to God. Adding 'for' clarifies the logical relationship between the clauses and makes the shift in subject from 'name' to 'he' (God) feel more natural to the English reader, improving flow while retaining the theological precision of 'he'.

revisions.from: they bring trouble down on me
revisions.to: they bring down trouble on me
revisions.rationale: While grammatically correct, the current word order splits the verb 'bring' from its particle 'down.' Rephrasing to keep the phrasal verb 'bring down' together results in a slightly more natural and idiomatic English flow for this type of metaphorical expression.

revisions.from: clings after you
revisions.to: follows hard after you
revisions.rationale: The current rendering combines the English verb 'to cling' with the preposition 'after.' The standard English idiom is 'to cling to,' which makes 'clings after' sound unnatural. While this is a laudably literal translation of the Hebrew construction (the verb דבק, 'to cling,' with the preposition אחר, 'after'), the resulting English phrase is awkward. A more idiomatic rendering like 'follows hard after you' would capture both the intensity of דבק and the pursuit of אחר in more natural English. Alternatively, 'clings close behind you' (from the draft's own footnote) would also be more fluid.

revisions.from: and establish the work of our hands upon us;
revisions.to: and establish for us the work of our hands;
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering of the second `עָלֵינוּ` as 'upon us' is awkward in English. While it formally parallels the first clause ('favor... upon us'), the phrase 'establish the work... upon us' is idiomatic in neither Hebrew nor English and its meaning is unclear. The preposition `עַל` here likely carries a dative sense, meaning 'for us' or 'for our benefit,' a rendering adopted by most major modern translations (NIV, ESV, NASB) for clarity. Translating it as 'for us' makes the meaning clear without any significant loss, which is preferable under an optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: the salvation of my face
revisions.to: the help of my countenance
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase 'יְשׁוּעֹת פָּנַי' is a dense idiom where God's salvation is described by its effect on the psalmist's face (i.e., lifting it from being downcast). The literal rendering 'the salvation of my face' is a calque that is not idiomatic in English and is likely to be confusing, as it implies the face itself possesses salvation. While preserving the source imagery is a good goal, in this case it creates an awkwardness that obscures the meaning. The suggested rewrite, a classic rendering already present in the translator's footnote, better translates the *meaning* of the idiom into clear and effective English, which is a better application of the optimal-equivalence philosophy here.

revisions.from: In place of your fathers, your sons will be
revisions.to: In place of your fathers will be your sons
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing, 'your sons will be,' is grammatically correct but stylistically awkward. The verb 'to be' at the end of the clause feels incomplete and can momentarily confuse the reader. The Hebrew word order (תַּחַת אֲבֹתֶיךָ יִהְיוּ בָנֶיךָ) naturally supports an English inversion, 'In place of your fathers will be your sons.' This alternative is more elegant, clearer, and avoids the dangling predicate while still closely reflecting the source structure.

revisions.from: the salvation of my face
revisions.to: the help of my countenance
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is a literal calque of the Hebrew construct phrase 'יְשׁוּעֹת פָּנַי'. While formally accurate, the resulting English 'the salvation of my face' is awkward and obscure. It does not clearly convey the intended meaning—that God's deliverance is what restores the psalmist's downcast expression. The suggested rewrite, 'the help of my countenance,' uses well-established lexical alternatives (also present in the footnote) that are more idiomatic in English. 'Help' for יְשׁוּעוֹת is common, and 'countenance' is a more fitting term than 'face' for this context of emotional expression, making the meaning clearer while still preserving the core imagery.

revisions.from: bereavement for my soul
revisions.to: my soul is bereaved
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase 'שְׁכוֹל לְנַפְשִׁי' is a nominal clause describing the result of the enemies' actions. The current rendering, 'bereavement for my soul,' is a literal but grammatically awkward fragment in English that obscures this relationship. It's not clear how this phrase connects to the preceding clause. Recasting it as a full clause ('my soul is bereaved') is a standard and effective translation technique for Hebrew nominal clauses, resulting in clear, idiomatic English that accurately conveys the psalmist's resulting state.

revisions.from: the Lord is among them—Sinai in holiness.
revisions.to: the Lord is among them as at Sinai, in holiness.
revisions.rationale: The current rendering preserves the Hebrew's elliptical nature to the point of obscurity in English. The phrase 'Sinai in holiness' is syntactically disconnected and its function as an appositive is unclear. The em-dash is insufficient to bridge the logical gap for the reader. Adding the comparative 'as at' makes the widely accepted meaning explicit, providing necessary clarity. This is a common solution (cf. KJV, NET notes) that better balances faithfulness with readability under an optimal-equivalence philosophy.

revisions.from: as they have waited for my life
revisions.to: as they lie in wait for my life
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing creates a slightly unnatural comparison in English. While 'as' is a valid translation for כַּאֲשֶׁר and 'have waited for' for קִוּוּ, the combination feels stilted. The Hebrew clause provides the manner or purpose for their watching. The suggested rewrite, 'as they lie in wait for my life,' uses a well-attested sense of קִוּוּ in a hostile context (as noted in footnote c) and creates a more natural parallel with 'they watch my steps,' clarifying that the watching is part of the act of lying in wait.

revisions.from: Edom in the Valley of Salt, twelve thousand
revisions.to: twelve thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew places the number 'twelve thousand' in apposition to 'Edom'. The current English rendering separates these two elements with a prepositional phrase ('in the Valley of Salt'), making the number feel like an abrupt, tacked-on piece of information. This construction is awkward and less clear than it could be. Rewording to 'twelve thousand Edomites' (or 'twelve thousand from Edom') directly connects the number to the people group, resulting in a more natural and immediately understandable English sentence.

revisions.from: for signs and for appointed times, and for days and years
revisions.to: for signs, for appointed times, and for days and years
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew presents a list of three purposes for the lights: (1) for signs, (2) for appointed times, and (3) for days and years (a single unit). The current English rendering 'for A and for B, and for C' is slightly awkward. Using standard English punctuation for a list of three items, 'for A, for B, and for C,' would be clearer and flow more naturally while still accurately reflecting the source.

revisions.from: in which their seed is
revisions.to: with their seed in it
revisions.rationale: The current rendering, while grammatically precise in linking 'their seed' back to 'trees', is stylistically awkward and formal in English. The Hebrew relative clause 'אֲשֶׁר זַרְעוֹ בוֹ' (which its seed is in it) can be rendered more naturally and idiomatically as 'with their seed in it', where 'it' clearly refers to the fruit. This change improves the flow of the verse without sacrificing accuracy.

revisions.from: made coverings for themselves
revisions.to: made themselves coverings
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is grammatically correct and follows the Hebrew word order. However, placing the indirect object before the direct object ('made themselves coverings') is a more common and slightly more fluid word order in contemporary English. This is a minor stylistic suggestion to improve flow.

revisions.from: It will cause thorns and thistles to grow
revisions.to: It will produce thorns and thistles
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is a very literal translation of the Hebrew Hiphil (causative) stem of צמח. While technically accurate, the construction 'cause ... to grow' is slightly wooden in English when the subject is land. A more idiomatic and forceful rendering like 'produce' or 'bring forth' (as seen in many major translations like the NIV and ESV) captures the causative sense more naturally.

revisions.from: from the ground
revisions.to: by the ground
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew preposition 'min' can mean 'from,' 'by,' or 'away from.' While 'from the ground' is a traditional rendering that preserves ambiguity (as noted in the footnote), it is slightly awkward in English. Given the immediate context personifying the ground as an active agent ('which has opened its mouth'), an instrumental reading ('by the ground') is contextually strong and provides a clearer, more forceful statement. The effect of this curse (separation from the ground's productivity) is then detailed in the following verse.

revisions.from: he called the name of the city after the name of his son
revisions.to: he named the city after his son
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is a very literal translation of the Hebrew idiom (קָרָא שֵׁם... כְּשֵׁם), which preserves the repetition of 'name' (שֵׁם) found in the source text. However, the resulting English phrase 'called the name... after the name...' is slightly unnatural. A more idiomatic rendering like 'he named the city after his son' (as noted in footnote a) or the slightly more formal 'he called the city by the name of his son' would improve the English flow while still conveying the meaning accurately.

revisions.from: the second
revisions.to: the other
revisions.rationale: While 'the second' is a literal translation of הַשֵּׁנִית, in English, when referring to the remaining member of a pair, 'the other' is more idiomatic. Since the context establishes there are exactly two wives, 'the other' is a more natural and equally accurate rendering. This is the choice made by most modern English translations (e.g., ESV, NRSV, NASB).

revisions.from: And Enosh lived, after he fathered Kenan, eight hundred and fifteen years
revisions.to: And Enosh lived eight hundred and fifteen years after he fathered Kenan
revisions.rationale: The current word order, while closely following the Hebrew syntax, creates a slightly awkward English sentence by separating the verb 'lived' from its adverbial phrase of duration ('eight hundred and fifteen years') with an intervening clause. Reordering the clauses to connect the verb and its duration more directly ('lived eight hundred and fifteen years after...') would improve the natural flow and readability without any loss of meaning.

revisions.from: Noah fathered
revisions.to: he fathered
revisions.rationale: The repetition of 'Noah' is a feature of Hebrew narrative style but is slightly redundant in English when the subject of the main clause is clear from the preceding subordinate clause. Using the pronoun 'he' would create a more natural and fluid English sentence without any loss of meaning.

revisions.from: lived, after he fathered Mahalalel, eight hundred and forty years,
revisions.to: lived eight hundred and forty years after he fathered Mahalalel,
revisions.rationale: The current word order, which closely follows the Hebrew, combined with the comma usage, makes the phrase 'after he fathered Mahalalel' feel like a parenthetical interruption. This awkwardly separates the verb 'lived' from its duration. Reordering the phrases to 'lived eight hundred and forty years after he fathered Mahalalel' creates a more natural and fluid English sentence, which is a common rendering strategy for this formulaic expression in Genesis.

revisions.from: from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat from it
revisions.to: from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew text uses a topicalization structure where the object is fronted for emphasis and then referred to again with a resumptive pronoun ('from the tree... you shall not eat from it'). While the draft's literal rendering is faithful to the source structure, the repetition is stylistically awkward in English. Removing the redundant final phrase 'from it' results in more natural English syntax while still preserving the emphasis of the fronted element.

revisions.from: And the LORD God built the rib that he had taken from the man into a woman
revisions.to: And the rib that the LORD God had taken from the man, he built into a woman
revisions.rationale: The current word order, while a direct and formal rendering of the Hebrew syntax (Verb-Object-Result), is slightly awkward in English. The construction 'built the rib... into a woman' can sound clunky. By fronting the object ('the rib... he built into a woman'), the sentence becomes more idiomatic and flows better. This syntactic adjustment is common in major modern translations (e.g., ESV, NRSVUE) and it preserves all the drafter's key lexical choices, including the deliberate use of 'built'.

revisions.from: You send out your Spirit, they are created
revisions.to: When you send out your Spirit, they are created
revisions.rationale: The current rendering uses a comma to join two independent clauses ('You send out your Spirit' and 'they are created'), which is a comma splice in formal English. This structure can feel awkward and slightly obscure the clear cause-and-effect relationship implied by the Hebrew syntax (consecutive imperfect verbs). Recasting the first clause as subordinate ('When you send...') resolves the grammatical issue and makes the logical connection more explicit and fluid for the English reader, a common and effective strategy for this Hebrew construction (cf. ESV, NIV).

revisions.from: when peoples gather together, and kingdoms to serve the LORD—
revisions.to: when peoples and kingdoms gather together to serve the LORD—
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew syntax presents 'peoples' (עַמִּים) and 'kingdoms' (וּמַמְלָכוֹת) as a compound subject of the infinitive 'to be gathered' (בְּהִקָּבֵץ). The current English rendering separates them, creating an awkward construction ('and kingdoms to serve the LORD') that makes 'kingdoms' seem detached from the gathering action. Combining the subjects ('when peoples and kingdoms gather together...') more accurately reflects the Hebrew grammar and results in a more natural and clear English sentence. A less disruptive alternative would be to add a comma after 'kingdoms' to clarify the parallel structure.

revisions.from: the words of his signs
revisions.to: the events of his signs
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering of the Hebrew `דִּבְרֵי אֹתוֹתָיו` as 'the words of his signs' is unnatural and potentially confusing in English, as signs are typically actions, not verbal statements. While the drafter's goal of transparency is noted, the result is awkward. The term `דִּבְרֵי` here idiomatically signifies 'matters' or 'events,' as correctly noted in the footnote. Using 'the events of his signs' or 'the details of his signs' in the main text would provide a clearer and more readable rendering that still captures the substance added by the word `דִּבְרֵי` without sacrificing the meaning.

revisions.from: Egypt was glad when they departed, for dread of them had fallen upon them.
revisions.to: The Egyptians were glad when they departed, for dread of them had fallen upon them.
revisions.rationale: The current rendering creates pronoun ambiguity. The first two pronouns ('they', 'them') refer to the Israelites, but the final 'them' refers to the Egyptians. This forces the reader to resolve a confusing construction. By translating the collective noun מִצְרַיִם as 'The Egyptians' instead of 'Egypt', the final pronoun 'them' gains a clear and grammatically consistent antecedent, resolving the ambiguity without altering the verse's meaning.

revisions.from: The earth is the LORD’s, and its fullness,
revisions.to: The earth and its fullness are the LORD’s,
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew presents 'the earth and its fullness' (הָאָרֶץ וּמְלוֹאָהּ) as a single, compound subject. The current draft splits this pair, creating a slightly awkward construction ('The earth is..., and its fullness'). Uniting them ('The earth and its fullness are...') not only results in more natural English grammar (using a plural verb for a compound subject) but also more faithfully mirrors the structure of the Hebrew poetic line.

revisions.from: Far from my deliverance are the words of my roaring.
revisions.to: Why are you so far from delivering me, from the words of my roaring?
revisions.rationale: The current rendering parses the difficult Hebrew of the second colon with 'the words of my roaring' as the subject. While a grammatically possible literal interpretation, this creates an awkward and abstract statement in English. The more common and contextually compelling interpretation sees this colon as continuing the question to God. This requires rendering the verbal noun יְשׁוּעָה as 'delivering me' and supplying the elided subject ('you') and interrogative force. This change makes the lament more direct and personal, focusing on God's distance, which is more in keeping with the tone of the opening line.

revisions.from: and I will add to all your praise.
revisions.to: and I will add still more to your praise.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew idiom וְהוֹסַפְתִּי עַל (wehosapti 'al), literally 'and I will add upon,' signifies an increase or continuation. The current rendering 'and I will add to all your praise' is a literalism that is slightly unnatural in English, potentially leaving the reader to question what is being added. The suggested rewrite clarifies the sense of increase ('still more') while retaining the verb 'add,' making the meaning more immediately apparent and idiomatic in English.

revisions.from: together, rich and poor.
revisions.to: rich and poor together.
revisions.rationale: The current placement of 'together' as a parenthetical, set off by commas, is syntactically awkward and disrupts the poetic flow. The Hebrew word יַחַד ('together' or 'alike') most likely modifies the entire group being addressed. Moving 'together' to the end of the line, as in '...rich and poor together,' is more natural in English and better captures the sense that all these groups are being addressed as one.

revisions.from: even they
revisions.to: even it
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses a plural pronoun (הֵם, 'they'), likely referring back to the plural noun 'eyes' (עֵינַי). However, in the English construction 'the light of my eyes,' the grammatical subject is the singular noun 'light.' Rendering הֵם as 'they' creates a subject-pronoun disagreement in English ('light... they...'), which is awkward and confusing. A better approach is to use a singular pronoun ('it' or 'that') that agrees with the English subject 'light,' reflecting the sense of the Hebrew idiom while maintaining correct English grammar. This is the common solution in most major English translations.

revisions.from: stand opposite my affliction
revisions.to: stand aloof from my affliction
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'stand opposite my affliction' is an overly literal rendering of 'מִנֶּגֶד נִגְעִי יַעֲמֹדוּ' that is awkward in English. The Hebrew idiom conveys the idea of standing apart or at a distance because of the affliction. 'Stand aloof from my affliction' is a common and effective rendering (cf. ESV, NASB) that captures the sense of deliberate social withdrawal idiomatically and flows more naturally. It also creates a nice contrast between the attitudinal distance ('aloof') in the first clause and the physical distance ('far away') in the second.

revisions.from: Of the LORD’s servant, of David.
revisions.to: Of the LORD’s servant, David.
revisions.rationale: The current rendering repeats 'of' to mirror the repeated Hebrew preposition 'lə'. While formally parallel, this is awkward in English and can obscure the appositional relationship between 'the LORD's servant' and 'David.' It could be misread as two separate attributions. Removing the second 'of' clarifies that David is the servant being referenced and results in more natural English syntax. This improved rendering is already noted as an alternative in the verse's footnotes.

revisions.from: who seek my life for ruin
revisions.to: who seek to destroy my life
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing is a literal but unidiomatic English rendering of the Hebrew `lə·šō·ʾāh yə·ḇaq·šū naf·šî`. The phrase 'seek [a life] for ruin' is not natural English. Combining the elements into a purpose infinitive ('seek to destroy my life') is a common and effective translation strategy that produces a much clearer and more natural expression while accurately conveying the Hebrew meaning.

revisions.from: A psalm, a song for the dedication of the house, of David.
revisions.to: A psalm. A song for the dedication of the house. Of David.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew superscription is a list of distinct descriptors: genre (psalm), occasion (song for dedication), and attribution (of David). The current draft's use of commas to string these into a single sentence is awkward, especially the final phrase ', of David,' which feels tacked on. Breaking the title into short, separate phrases punctuated with periods better reflects the nature of a title as a list of labels and is a common, clear solution in modern English translations. This improves readability without changing the excellent lexical choices.

revisions.from: For the choir director: according to The Gittith. A psalm of David.
revisions.to: For the choir director; according to the Gittith. A psalm of David.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew superscription is a single, multi-part title. The current punctuation, with a colon followed by a period, breaks the musical instructions into two separate sentences, which feels disjointed. Using a semicolon better links the related instructions ('For the choir director; according to the Gittith') before the main title ('A psalm of David'). Additionally, capitalizing the definite article in 'The Gittith' is non-standard; 'the Gittith' is more natural.

revisions.from: I kept quiet from good
revisions.to: I kept silent, even from good
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'I kept quiet from good' is unidiomatic and obscure in English. While 'from good' is a literal translation of מִטּוֹב, the context strongly suggests the psalmist refrained from speaking even good things. The preposition מִן often has a wider semantic range than just 'from'. Rendering it as 'even from good' (cf. NASB, KJV) is a common and contextually sensitive solution that is much clearer to the English reader without being overly interpretive. The current draft's literalism creates unnecessary awkwardness that the footnote then has to resolve.

revisions.from: Please, LORD, save us, please! Please, LORD, grant success, please!
revisions.to: Please, LORD, save us now! Please, LORD, grant success now!
revisions.rationale: The current rendering 'Please... please!' is a direct mapping of the Hebrew particles `אָנָּא` and `נָּא`. While this conveys intensity, the repetition of the same word is unnatural in English and can create a slightly juvenile tone. While the drafter noted that `נָּא` here is more about intensification than time, the English word 'now' attached to an imperative often carries a sense of urgent plea ('Come on, do it now!') rather than being strictly temporal. Using 'now' for `נָּא` is a valid translation choice that results in a more idiomatic and powerful English phrase ('Please... now!') while still reflecting the force of both Hebrew particles.

revisions.from: For what vanity you have created all the sons of man.
revisions.to: For what vanity have you created all the sons of man?
revisions.rationale: The current word order, which follows the Hebrew, can be read as a declarative statement in English ('You created them for the purpose of vanity'). Inverting the subject and verb ('have you created') and adding a question mark makes it unambiguously a rhetorical question, which is the clear intent of the Hebrew 'עַל מַה־שָּׁוְא'. This better captures the psalmist's questioning lament.

revisions.from: Myrrh and aloes and cassia are all your garments
revisions.to: All your garments are fragrant with myrrh, aloes, and cassia
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew is a verbless clause juxtaposing a list of spices with 'all your garments.' The draft's rendering supplies a copula ('are') that results in an awkward English sentence, implying the garments are literally made of spices. While the meaning can be inferred, rephrasing to make the garments the subject and describing their quality ('are fragrant with...') is a common and much clearer solution that accurately conveys the intended sense without being overly interpretive.

revisions.from: He was known
revisions.to: One was known
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew verb is 3rd person masculine singular, but the context of the psalm refers to plural enemies. Using 'He' in English creates ambiguity, as it implies a specific antecedent that is not present. A generic rendering like 'One was known' or an impersonal 'It was seen as' would better capture the sense of the simile without causing confusion for the reader.

revisions.from: into a thicket of trees
revisions.to: in a thicket of trees
revisions.rationale: The preposition 'into' creates a slightly illogical image, as if the axes are being raised up into the branches from below. The Hebrew preposition 'בְּ' more likely means the action takes place 'in' the thicket or is directed 'against' it. Translating as 'in a thicket' clarifies that the woodcutter is standing within the forest, which is the more natural picture.

revisions.from: Shepherd of Israel, give ear—you who lead Joseph like a flock, you who are enthroned above the cherubim, shine forth.
revisions.to: Shepherd of Israel, who leads Joseph like a flock, give ear; you who are enthroned above the cherubim, shine forth.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew text, as structured by the Masoretic accents (specifically the atnach on 'Joseph'), presents two distinct, parallel petitions. The current draft combines these into a single, long sentence. This construction is syntactically awkward, separating the final imperative ('shine forth') from its governing address by two long clauses, and it obscures the elegant parallelism of the original poem. Rewriting this as two balanced clauses restores the poetic structure and improves readability.

revisions.from: my adversaries and my enemies against me
revisions.to: my adversaries and my enemies
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase is `צָרַי וְאֹיְבַי לִי`. The possessive suffixes in `צָרַי` (my adversaries) and `אֹיְבַי` (my enemies) already establish the hostile relationship to the psalmist. Rendering the dative `לִי` as an additional 'against me' is tautological in English and makes the line clunky. The force of the `לִי` is sufficiently captured by the possessive 'my'.

revisions.from: you brought those who rose against me down under me
revisions.to: you subdued those who rose against me beneath me
revisions.rationale: The current rendering combines the phrasal verb 'brought down' with the preposition 'under me,' creating a slightly redundant and awkward phrase ('down under me'). While 'brought down' is a valid translation for the verb תַּכְרִיעַ, an alternative like 'subdued' works more smoothly with the following prepositional phrase תַּחְתָּי ('beneath me' or 'under me'). This results in a more natural and powerful English sentence that maintains all the components of the Hebrew in a more elegant construction.

revisions.from: For a cup is in the hand of the LORD, and the wine foams, full of mixed spices; he pours from it
revisions.to: For a cup is in the hand of the LORD. The wine foams, full of mixed spices; he pours from it
revisions.rationale: The current punctuation creates a single, long sentence that is grammatically awkward and runs together distinct thoughts. The Hebrew text has a major syntactic break (marked by the `atnach` accent) after 'LORD'. Using a period to create two sentences better reflects the structure of the source text, improves readability, and resolves the incorrect use of a semicolon after 'spices' (which separates a phrase, not an independent clause).

revisions.from: darkness is my acquaintances
revisions.to: my acquaintances are darkness
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase `מְיֻדָּעַי מַחְשָׁךְ` is a verbless clause where the subject ('my acquaintances') most naturally precedes the predicate ('darkness'). The draft inverts this to make 'darkness' the subject. While this interpretive choice is common (e.g., 'darkness is my only friend'), the specific rendering 'darkness is my acquaintances' is awkward in English, equating a singular subject with a plural predicate nominative. A more direct translation, 'my acquaintances are darkness,' is faithful to the Hebrew syntax and word order, and is arguably just as metaphorically powerful, suggesting that the psalmist's companions have become synonymous with darkness itself.

revisions.from: His likeness is like a lion
revisions.to: He is like a lion
revisions.rationale: The draft's rendering of the opening nominal clause `דִּמְיֹנוֹ כְּאַרְיֵה` (lit. 'his likeness [is] like a lion') is overly literal, resulting in the redundant and awkward English phrase 'His likeness is like a lion.' The force of the Hebrew comparison is on the person being described, not on their abstract 'likeness.' A more idiomatic and clear rendering, as noted in footnote 'a', is 'He is like a lion.' This better captures the sense of the simile in natural English.

revisions.from: lest, if you are silent to me,
revisions.to: lest you be silent to me and
revisions.rationale: The construction 'lest, if' is stylistically awkward and redundant in English. The Hebrew particle 'pen' introduces a feared outcome that encompasses both God's silence and the psalmist's subsequent fate. A more direct rendering like 'lest you be silent... and I become...' connects these two linked consequences more smoothly, reflecting the Hebrew syntax where 'wə-' (and) joins the final two clauses.

revisions.from: blessings of good
revisions.to: blessings of goodness
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'blessings of good' is a very literal rendering of the Hebrew, but it is stylistically awkward in English. It can sound like a category rather than a quality. 'Blessings of goodness' is more idiomatic and natural-sounding, while remaining very close to the form of the source text. This rendering is found in other formal translations (e.g., NASB) and improves flow without resorting to a more dynamic interpretation like 'rich blessings'.

revisions.from: turn their back; with your bowstrings you will aim
revisions.to: turn their back when you aim your bowstrings
revisions.rationale: The current rendering presents two seemingly contradictory actions as parallel: the enemies turn their backs in flight, while God aims at their faces. This juxtaposition, marked by a semicolon, can be confusing for the English reader. The underlying Hebrew poetic structure likely implies a causal relationship. Making this relationship explicit (e.g., with 'when') resolves the logical tension and clarifies that the act of aiming is what causes the enemy to flee. This creates a more coherent and powerful image of the event.

revisions.from: their net’s pit
revisions.to: the pit for their net
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew construct שַׁחַת רִשְׁתָּם ('pit of their net') is difficult. The draft's rendering, 'their net’s pit,' is a literalism that is opaque and awkward in English, as it is unclear what a 'net's pit' is. While the footnote helpfully explains the ambiguity, the main text would be clearer and more communicative if it reflected the likely meaning: a pit dug in order to set a net trap. 'The pit for their net' clarifies this relationship for the reader, which is consistent with the verb 'dug' in the second clause and is a common scholarly interpretation.

revisions.from: Awake and rise up for my justice, my God and my Lord, for my cause.
revisions.to: Awake and rise up for my justice and for my cause, my God and my Lord.
revisions.rationale: The current word order places the vocative ('my God and my Lord') between two parallel prepositional phrases ('for my justice' and 'for my cause'). This is syntactically awkward in English and slightly obscures the poetic parallelism present in the Hebrew. Reordering the phrases to group the parallel elements ('for my justice and for my cause') and placing the vocative at the end creates a more natural, forceful, and rhetorically effective sentence that better reflects the structure of the original.

revisions.from: your righteousness, your praise all day long
revisions.to: your righteousness, and all day long of your praise
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is syntactically awkward. The verb phrase 'will speak of' governs both 'righteousness' and 'praise', but the preposition 'of' is omitted before the second object, creating an unidiomatic list. Additionally, the Hebrew poetic structure (וּלְשׁוֹנִי תֶהְגֶּה צִדְקֶךָ // כָּל־הַיּוֹם תְּהִלָּתֶךָ) places the temporal phrase 'all day long' at the start of the second clause, emphasizing its connection to 'your praise'. The suggested rewrite restores both the grammatical parallelism (by implying 'speak of your praise') and the poetic structure of the Hebrew, resulting in a more elegant and faithful translation.

revisions.from: and because of the curse and the lie that they tell
revisions.to: for the curse and the lie that they tell
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew presents two parallel reasons for the imprecation: their pride (בִגְאוֹנָם) and their deceitful speech (מֵאָלָה וּמִכַּחַשׁ). The current rendering, '...in their pride, and because of the curse...', creates a slightly disjointed coordination. Rephrasing to '...in their pride for the curse...' or '...in their pride, and for the curse...' would better reflect the parallel structure of the reasons for their downfall, making the English flow more logically and poetically.

revisions.from: above Jerusalem
revisions.to: at Jerusalem
revisions.rationale: The rendering 'above Jerusalem' for 'עַל יְרוּשָׁלִָם' is an overly literal translation. While the temple was on a higher elevation (Mount Zion), the standard and more natural English idiom is 'at Jerusalem'. The current phrasing is awkward and could be misinterpreted as the temple floating over the city rather than being situated within it on a high point. 'At Jerusalem' is a common locative sense for 'עַל' and is the consensus reading of major English translations, improving clarity and flow without sacrificing essential meaning.

revisions.from: For you have delivered my soul from death—have you not also my feet from stumbling?—that I may walk before God in the light of life.
revisions.to: For you have delivered my soul from death; have you not also kept my feet from stumbling, that I may walk before God in the light of life?
revisions.rationale: The current punctuation, with a question enclosed in em-dashes in the middle of a sentence, is grammatically awkward and disrupts the logical flow. It makes the 'that I may walk...' clause's antecedent unclear. The Hebrew accentuation (atnach on 'death') supports a major break, making two linked clauses more natural. Using a semicolon creates two clear, linked clauses, preserving the rhetorical question while ensuring the purpose clause ('that I may walk...') correctly modifies the deliverance of the feet from stumbling.

revisions.from: O LORD, for length of days
revisions.to: for length of days, O LORD.
revisions.rationale: The current word order places the vocative 'O LORD' in the middle of a thought, separating the predicate 'holiness befits your house' from its temporal modifier 'for length of days.' This is grammatically awkward and slightly obscures the connection. In the Hebrew, the phrase 'לְאֹרֶךְ יָמִים' (for length of days) most naturally modifies the preceding clause about the house's holiness. Placing the vocative at the very end of the verse ('...for length of days, O LORD.') maintains the vocative force while creating a more natural and clear English sentence.

revisions.from: and do not go out
revisions.to: and do you not go out
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew construction is a single rhetorical question governed by 'hă/lōʾ': 'Is it not [the case that] you have rejected us AND [that] you do not go out with our armies?' The current English rendering, '...and do not go out...', breaks the interrogative syntax and can be misread as a negative command. Restoring the interrogative form with 'and do you not go out' makes the English grammatical and accurately reflects the function of the Hebrew imperfect verb (tēṣēʾ) as part of the ongoing question.

revisions.from: They pour out words; they speak arrogantly;
revisions.to: They gush with words and speak arrogantly;
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew verse structure consists of two main parts, marked by the 'athnah. The first part, 'yabbîʿû yəḏabbərû ʿāṯāq', contains two parallel verbs describing a single complex action. The draft's rendering with a semicolon breaks this poetic unit into two disconnected clauses. Connecting the verbs with 'and' better reflects the Hebrew parallelism. Additionally, 'yabbîʿû' has a sense of 'gushing' or 'bubbling up' which is more vivid than 'pour out'; 'gush with words' captures this force well, using a word ('gush') already noted as an alternative by the drafter.

revisions.from: The heavens are the LORD’s heavens
revisions.to: The heavens—the heavens belong to the LORD
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses a casus pendens construction with repetition for emphasis (הַשָּׁמַיִם שָׁמַיִם לַיהוָה, lit. 'The heavens, heavens are for the LORD'). The current rendering forces this into a predicate nominative structure ('The heavens are the LORD’s heavens') which is awkward and slightly tautological in English. The suggested rewrite, which is similar to the alternative noted in the footnote, better captures the emphatic, topic-comment structure of the Hebrew and is clearer.

revisions.from: and the cave that was in it
revisions.to: and the cave within it
revisions.rationale: While the current rendering is grammatically correct and a literal translation of the Hebrew, 'and the cave within it' is more concise and stylistically smoother in English. It conveys the identical meaning with better flow.

revisions.from: to show the way before him to Goshen
revisions.to: to get directions before him to Goshen
revisions.rationale: The current rendering implies that Judah is the one who will be showing the way. This is contextually illogical, as he has just been sent to Joseph, presumably to make arrangements. The Hebrew is compressed, but the most natural reading is that the purpose of sending Judah is to get instructions from Joseph. The suggested rewrite, 'to get directions,' clarifies that Judah is the recipient of the instruction, which resolves the awkwardness while remaining faithful to the meaning of the verb לְהוֹרֹת ('to instruct/direct').

revisions.from: will answer for Pharaoh’s well-being
revisions.to: will give an answer for Pharaoh’s well-being
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing 'answer for...' is unidiomatic English in this context. It could imply answering 'on behalf of' Pharaoh's well-being. The Hebrew idiom here, 'ענה את שלום' (literally 'answer the peace/welfare of'), is better rendered by slightly unpacking the verb-object combination to improve clarity. The suggested rewrite, which is very close to the excellent rendering in the footnote ('give Pharaoh an answer of peace'), makes the meaning clear—that God will provide a response that concerns Pharaoh's welfare—while using more natural English phrasing.

revisions.from: seemed good in Pharaoh’s eyes and in the eyes of all his servants
revisions.to: seemed good to Pharaoh and all his servants
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering of the Hebrew parallelism `בְּעֵינֵי... וּבְעֵינֵי...` (in the eyes of... and in the eyes of...) is slightly redundant in English. A more natural and common rendering of this idiom is 'seemed good to...' or 'pleased...'. For example, 'The proposal seemed good to Pharaoh and all his servants' would be more idiomatic while retaining the same meaning.

revisions.from: for badness
revisions.to: for ugliness
revisions.rationale: The current rendering 'for badness' is an overly literal translation of the Hebrew `לָרֹעַ`. This results in an awkward English phrase that could be misinterpreted as referring to a moral quality. The context is clearly about the cows' physical appearance and condition. Using 'for ugliness' is more natural, avoids ambiguity, and creates a clear consistency with the earlier phrase `רָעוֹת תֹּאַר` ('ugly in appearance'), which comes from the same root. The suggested alternative is already present in the footnote, indicating its viability.

revisions.from: are coming of great abundance
revisions.to: of great abundance are coming
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing, 'seven years are coming of great abundance,' is unidiomatic English. The preposition 'of' does not function correctly to link the verb phrase 'are coming' with the noun 'abundance' in this construction. A more natural and clear rendering would be 'seven years of great abundance are coming,' which makes 'of great abundance' a standard adjectival phrase modifying 'years'. This small change significantly improves readability without altering the core meaning or lexical choices.

revisions.from: Abraham was buried, and Sarah his wife
revisions.to: Abraham and Sarah his wife were buried
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses a singular verb (קֻבַּר, 'was buried') preceding a compound subject ('Abraham and Sarah his wife'), which is standard Hebrew grammar. The current English rendering mimics this structure, resulting in an awkward and slightly ambiguous phrase that could imply only Abraham was buried. A more natural and clear English translation would treat 'Abraham and Sarah his wife' as a compound subject requiring a plural verb ('were buried'), which accurately conveys that the act of burial applied to both individuals.

revisions.from: the purchase of the field and the cave that is in it, from the sons of Heth.
revisions.to: The purchase of the field and the cave that is in it was from the sons of Heth.
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew text is a nominal clause (Subject + Predicate), which functions as a complete sentence. The English draft renders this as a sentence fragment by omitting the implied copula ('was'). This is grammatically awkward and less clear than the Hebrew. Supplying the verb 'was' creates a complete English sentence that accurately reflects the grammatical function of the source text, which is a standard practice in translating Hebrew nominal clauses.

revisions.from: in exchange for all their livestock
revisions.to: for all their livestock
revisions.rationale: The phrase 'in exchange for' is used twice in the verse, making the English slightly repetitive. Since the first instance clearly establishes the nature of the transaction, the second instance can be rendered more concisely as 'for all their livestock' without any loss of meaning, improving the sentence's flow.

revisions.from: And it happened, as he drew back his hand, that behold,
revisions.to: And as he drew back his hand, behold,
revisions.rationale: The current rendering attempts to translate both the introductory `וַיְהִי` ('And it happened') and the particle `וְהִנֵּה` ('that behold'), resulting in the syntactically awkward English phrase 'it happened that behold'. The suggested rewrite removes the formulaic and somewhat redundant 'And it happened,' which improves the narrative flow while still faithfully rendering the temporal clause ('as he drew back his hand') and the marker of surprise, 'behold' (`הִנֵּה`).

revisions.from: every one among the goats that is not speckled or spotted, and among the lambs that is not dark-colored
revisions.to: any goat that is not speckled or spotted and any lamb that is not dark-colored
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing is grammatically cumbersome. 'Every one among the goats' is less direct than 'any goat,' and the second clause ('and among the lambs...') is an awkward elliptical construction. Restructuring as two clear, parallel clauses ('any goat... and any lamb...') is more concise and idiomatic in English.

revisions.from: and their camels were carrying gum, balm, and myrrh, going to take them down to Egypt.
revisions.to: with their camels carrying gum, balm, and myrrh, on their way to take it down to Egypt.
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing creates a dangling participle ('going') that ambiguously, and likely incorrectly, attaches to 'camels' as the nearest subject. The Hebrew participle הוֹלְכִים ('going') refers to the masculine plural Ishmaelites, not the camels. The suggested rewrite clarifies that the Ishmaelites are the agents of travel by restructuring the circumstantial clause, which is a common and effective way to render this Hebrew construction in clear English. Using 'it' for the collective cargo is also more idiomatic.

revisions.from: and he appointed him over his house and put all that he had into his hand
revisions.to: and his master appointed him over his house and put all that he had into his care.
revisions.rationale: The English rendering creates a chain of pronouns ('he', 'him', 'his', 'he', 'his') that is ambiguous. The subject of 'appointed' is not immediately clear, and 'into his hand' could refer to Potiphar's own hand, though context suggests Joseph's. While the Hebrew structure is preserved, the English pronoun system makes this construction more confusing than the source. A clarification, such as specifying the subject ('his master' or 'Potiphar') or using a clearer English idiom for the final phrase ('into his care' or 'into Joseph's hand'), would improve readability without altering the meaning.

revisions.from: It is as I said to you, saying,
revisions.to: It is just as I told you:
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is a very literal translation of the Hebrew structure `הוּא אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתִּי ... לֵאמֹר`. This results in the slightly redundant and awkward English phrase 'I said... saying'. While defensible as a formal equivalent, a more optimal-equivalence rendering would smooth this Hebraism. Using a colon is a standard English way to represent the function of `לֵאמֹר` (introducing a quote). Additionally, 'just as I told you' is slightly more forceful and natural than 'as I said to you,' better capturing the emphatic `הוּא אֲשֶׁר` ('It is that which...').

revisions.from: and you go, bring grain for the famine of your households
revisions.to: but you, go and bring grain for your hungry households
revisions.rationale: The current rendering of the second clause is syntactically stilted. 1) The Hebrew `וְאַתֶּם` ('and you') creates a contrast with the brother who stays, which is often better captured by 'but you' than 'and you'. 2) The consecutive imperatives `לְכוּ הָבִיאוּ` ('go, bring') are more idiomatically rendered in English as 'go and bring'. 3) The phrase `רַעֲבוֹן בָּתֵּיכֶם` ('the famine of your households') is a literalism that sounds unnatural. 'Your hungry households' is a more dynamic and idiomatic equivalent that captures the meaning effectively.

revisions.from: And it happened, as they were emptying their sacks, that behold,
revisions.to: And as they were emptying their sacks, behold,
revisions.rationale: The construction 'And it happened... that behold' is an awkward calque of the Hebrew narrative sequence וַיְהִי... וְהִנֵּה. The phrase 'that behold' is unidiomatic in English. Rephrasing the opening to 'And as they were emptying their sacks, behold,' preserves the temporal relationship established by the participle (מְרִיקִים) and the element of surprise from הִנֵּה, but in a more natural English style.

revisions.from: do not let your eye spare your belongings
revisions.to: do not be concerned about your belongings
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew idiom 'let not your eye spare/pity' (וְעֵינְכֶם אַל־תָּחֹס) is rendered too literally, creating an awkward and unnatural English phrase. The verb 'spare' does not collocate with 'eye' in this manner in English. The intended meaning is about not feeling regret or anxiety over leaving possessions behind. The suggested rewrite, which aligns with the provided footnote and many modern translations (e.g., ESV, NIV), conveys this meaning clearly and naturally to the English reader.

revisions.from: and go; come
revisions.to: and go back
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew uses two consecutive imperatives, `לְכוּ` (go/depart) and `בֹאוּ` (come/arrive), to describe the single journey from Egypt to Canaan. The current rendering 'and go; come' is a very literal but stylistically awkward English construction. The semicolon creates an unnatural separation between two tightly linked actions. A more idiomatic and clear rendering in English would be to combine the sense of both verbs into a single phrasal verb like 'go back' or 'return,' which accurately conveys the meaning of leaving one place and arriving at a previous one without the clunky phrasing. The full phrase would then read '...and go back to the land of Canaan.'

revisions.from: And Laban gave Zilpah, his female servant, to Leah his daughter as a female servant.
revisions.to: And Laban gave Zilpah, his female servant, to Leah his daughter to be her servant.
revisions.rationale: The direct translation of the repeated Hebrew word 'šip̄ḥāh' results in the awkward repetition of 'female servant' in English. While formally accurate, it is stylistically poor. Changing the final phrase from 'as a female servant' to the infinitive clause 'to be her servant' is a more natural English idiom for this Hebrew construction (a second accusative indicating role/purpose). This change resolves the awkwardness, improves readability, and does not alter the essential meaning.

revisions.from: Whoever it is found with among your servants
revisions.to: Whichever of your servants it is found with
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing, 'Whoever it is found with,' is a grammatically awkward construction in English. While understandable, the word order is unnatural for a passive clause. The suggested rewrite, 'Whichever of your servants it is found with,' is more idiomatic and clearly integrates the partitive sense of 'מֵעֲבָדֶיךָ' ('from/among your servants') with the relative pronoun, resulting in a smoother and more polished sentence.

revisions.from: And God Almighty may he bless you
revisions.to: And may God Almighty bless you
revisions.rationale: The current phrasing follows a Hebrew syntactic pattern (fronting the subject, then using a verb with an implicit or explicit pronoun) that is unnatural and awkward in English. It creates a slight redundancy with 'God Almighty' and 'he'. Restructuring the clause to 'And may God Almighty bless you' preserves the intended jussive mood of the blessing while using a standard and more elegant English construction.

revisions.from: and hold him by your hand
revisions.to: and hold him fast with your hand
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew phrase `וְהַחֲזִיקִי אֶת יָדֵךְ בּוֹ` literally translates to 'and make your hand strong on him,' which is an idiom for providing firm support. The current rendering, 'hold him by your hand,' is ambiguous in English and could be misinterpreted as simply 'hold his hand.' This misses the emphasis on strength and support inherent in the verb `חזק`. The suggested rewrite, 'hold him fast with your hand,' better captures the force of the Hebrew idiom and aligns with the context of supporting a boy who has collapsed.

revisions.from: Why is this that
revisions.to: Why is it that
revisions.rationale: The current rendering 'Why is this that' is a calque of the Hebrew `לָמָּה זֶּה` that is unidiomatic and awkward in English. While the drafter's goal of preserving 'Hebrew texture' is noted, this choice hinders readability. A more natural English emphatic form like 'Why is it that' captures the emphasis of `זֶה` without sounding like a translation error. It serves as an excellent middle ground between the overly literal draft and the smoothed-out footnote, preserving emphasis while being idiomatic.

revisions.from: and I will do good with you
revisions.to: and I will deal well with you
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is an overly literal translation of the Hebrew idiom (Hiphil of יטב with עִם). In English, 'do good with you' is unnatural and could be misconstrued as 'perform good deeds alongside you.' A more idiomatic and clearer rendering that captures the sense of bestowing favor is 'deal well with you' or 'do you good.' While the drafter's choice to preserve the preposition 'with' is noted, optimal equivalence should favor a natural target-language expression when a literal one is awkward.

revisions.from: acts of covenant faithfulness and all the faithfulness
revisions.to: acts of steadfast love and all the faithfulness
revisions.rationale: The current rendering translates two distinct Hebrew words, חֶסֶד (ḥesed) and אֱמֶת (’emet), using variations of the same English word ('faithfulness'). This creates a stylistically awkward and potentially confusing repetition. While the lexical choice for חֶסֶד as 'covenant faithfulness' is strong in isolation, it functions poorly in this classic pairing. The suggested rewrite uses the standard and well-regarded pairing of 'steadfast love and faithfulness,' which maintains the distinction between the two Hebrew concepts. It also preserves the drafter's correct insight that the plural חֲסָדִים refers to concrete actions by keeping the word 'acts'.

revisions.from: ‘Thus says your servant Jacob, “I have sojourned with Laban and have remained until now.”’”
revisions.to: ‘Thus says your servant Jacob: I have sojourned with Laban and have remained until now.’”
revisions.rationale: The use of three nested levels of quotation marks is grammatically complex and visually confusing for the reader. A more conventional and clearer way to punctuate this in English is to use a colon to introduce the final piece of the message, which avoids the third layer of quotation marks while preserving the structure of the reported speech.

revisions.from: the sinew of the hip that is on the socket of the thigh
revisions.to: the sciatic tendon, which is on the socket of the thigh,
revisions.rationale: The current rendering is confusingly redundant. By translating 'gid ha-nasheh' as 'sinew of the hip' (a locational term) and immediately following it with 'that is on the socket of the thigh' (another locational term), the text becomes awkward. Adopting the strong traditional interpretation of 'gid ha-nasheh' as the 'sciatic tendon' (already mentioned in footnote 'b') resolves this. It identifies the tendon by its specific name, which is then located by the relative clause, making the sentence clearer and more readable.

revisions.from: that God had created to do
revisions.to: that God had created and made
revisions.rationale: The literal rendering of the Hebrew phrase אֲשֶׁר בָּרָא אֱלֹהִים לַעֲשׂוֹת is syntactically difficult and semantically unclear in English. While faithful to the form, it obscures the meaning for the reader. A common and contextually sound interpretation treats the two verbs (create, do/make) as a hendiadys, signifying the totality of the creative work. The suggested rewrite, 'that God had created and made,' is a classic rendering that resolves the awkwardness into fluent English while capturing the sense of completed divine action. Since the footnote already explains the grammatical difficulty, the main text would benefit from this clearer phrasing, which better aligns with an 'optimal-equivalence' philosophy.

revisions.from: to my flesh
revisions.to: to my skin
revisions.rationale: The current rendering 'my bones cling to my flesh' is a confusing image in English, as bones are naturally within flesh. The Hebrew word בָּשָׂר (bāśār) can mean 'skin' in contexts of extreme emaciation, and major lexicons (like HALOT) specifically cite Psalm 102:6 as an example of this usage. Translating it as 'to my skin' creates a much clearer and more powerful image of the psalmist's skeletal state, which is a common idiom in English. While the drafter's choice is based on the primary meaning of 'flesh,' the context strongly favors the secondary meaning 'skin' for coherence and impact.

revisions.from: iron came upon his soul
revisions.to: his soul came into iron
revisions.rationale: The current rendering assumes 'iron' (בַּרְזֶל, a masculine noun) is the subject of the verb 'came' (בָּאָה, a feminine form). This creates a significant grammatical gender disagreement. The more natural reading of the Hebrew syntax takes 'his soul' (נַפְשׁוֹ, from the feminine noun נֶפֶשׁ) as the subject, which correctly agrees with the feminine verb. This yields 'his soul came into iron,' a grammatically sound and poetically powerful rendering that shifts the focus from the instrument's action to the person's experience of entering into bondage.

revisions.from: My voice is to God
revisions.to: I lift my voice to God
revisions.rationale: The Hebrew verbless clause 'קוֹלִי אֶל אֱלֹהִים' implies an action of crying or directing one's voice. The current rendering, 'My voice is to God,' is overly literal and uses a static copula ('is') that makes the phrase awkward and inert in English. It sounds like a statement of possession or classification rather than the beginning of an urgent cry.
